---
title: "5.3.1 Cyber security threats"
subtitle: "Threats overview, brute force attacks and data interception · P189-P190"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.72em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)

## Why Cyber Security Matters

### Keeping data safe

Keeping data safe is extremely important for many reasons.

It may be personal data that you want to keep within your family or close friends, or commercial data, such as <span class="mark">passwords and bank details</span>.

Data can be corrupted or deleted either through accidental damage or malicious acts.

## Threat Categories

### Section 5.3.1 list

The textbook considers these cyber threats:

::: {.tight-list}
- brute force attacks
- data interception
- distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks
- hacking
- malware: viruses, worms, Trojan horse, spyware, adware and ransomware
- phishing, pharming and social engineering
:::

## Brute Force Attacks

### Trying all combinations

If a hacker wants to "crack" a password, they can systematically try all the different combinations of letters, numbers and other symbols until eventually they find the password.

This is known as a <span class="term">brute force attack</span> (暴力破解攻击). There is not a lot of sophistication in the technique.

## Common Passwords

### First logical step

One way to reduce the number of attempts needed is to check whether the password is one of the most common ones used.

The textbook examples are:

::: {.tight-list}
- `123456`
- `password`
- `qwerty`
- `111111`
- `abc123`
:::

## Word Lists

### Faster than total trial and error

If the password is not in the common password list, the next step is to start with a <span class="term">word list</span>.

A word list is a text file containing a collection of words that can be used in a brute force attack.

The longer a password is and the greater the variation of characters used, the <span class="mark">harder it will be to crack</span>.

## Data Interception

### Stealing data in transit

<span class="term">Data interception</span> (数据拦截) is a form of stealing data by tapping into a wired or wireless communication link.

The intent is to compromise privacy or to obtain confidential information.

## Packet Sniffing

### Wired networks

Interception can be carried out using a <span class="term">packet sniffer</span>, which examines data packets being sent over a network.

The intercepted data is sent back to the hacker. This is a common method when wired networks are used.

## Wardriving

### Wireless interception

Wi-Fi data interception can be carried out using <span class="term">wardriving</span>, sometimes called Access Point Mapping.

Using this method, data can be intercepted using a laptop or smartphone, antenna and a GPS device, together with software, outside a building or somebody's house.

The intercepted Wi-Fi signal can reveal personal data, often without the user being aware.

## Reducing Interception Risk

### Encryption and safer Wi-Fi use

Encryption does not stop the data being intercepted or altered, but it makes the data <span class="mark">incomprehensible</span> if the hacker does not have the decryption key.

The textbook recommends:

::: {.tight-list}
- WEP encryption protocol together with a firewall against wardriving
- complex passwords to protect the wireless router
- not using Wi-Fi connectivity in public places such as airports
:::

## Classroom Check

### Brute force and interception

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- brute force attacks systematically try combinations until a password is found
- common passwords and word lists reduce the number of attempts needed
- longer passwords with more varied characters are harder to crack
- data interception steals data by tapping wired or wireless links
- packet sniffers examine data packets; wardriving targets Wi-Fi signals
- encryption makes intercepted data incomprehensible without the decryption key
:::

## Bridge

### Next: DDoS and hacking

The next deck explains attacks that stop access to services and illegal access to systems.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)
